impacts.caseName: Teva Pharmaceuticals International GmbH v. Eli Lilly and Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in granting judgment as a matter of law that Teva's anti-CGRP antibody headache patents were invalid for failure to satisfy the written-description and enablement requirements of 35 U.S.C. § 112.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's JMOL of invalidity of Teva's headache patents covering methods of treating headache using humanized anti-CGRP antagonist antibodies, finding that Teva had satisfied the written-description and enablement requirements under § 112. The jury had found willful infringement by Lilly and no invalidity. This restores a significant patent verdict protecting Teva's Ajovy franchise against Lilly's competing migraine treatments.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the pharmaceutical and biologics space benefit from a reinforced standard for written description and enablement, particularly where specifications disclose one species and known methods for making others within a claimed genus.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors developing biosimilar or follow-on biologic products face increased risk that broad genus claims covering antibody therapies will be upheld, potentially limiting market entry.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder that conditions patent licenses on exclusive purchase of its unpatented products engages in unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the 'nonstaple goods' defense shields such conduct when the tied products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act for conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the finding that Ingevity's honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems outside the patent's scope), defeating the 'nonstaple goods' defense.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors benefit from clearer antitrust protection against patent holders who leverage patent rights to foreclose competition in adjacent unpatented product markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that bundle patent licenses with product purchase requirements face significant antitrust liability risk, particularly where the tied products have uses outside the patent's scope.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods for detecting anomalous program executions using an emulator and a model of function calls are directed to abstract ideas under Alice step one of the 35 U.S.C. § 101 analysis.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted claims of the '322 and '115 patents directed to detecting anomalous program executions were abstract at Alice step one, requiring remand for step two analysis. The court also ruled that damages based on Norton's foreign sales were improperly included, and separately reversed a contempt order against Norton's counsel Quinn Emanuel.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against cybersecurity patent assertions benefit from the court's willingness to find claims directed to detecting malicious software abstract at Alice step one, and from the ruling that foreign sales cannot automatically be included in domestic patent damages.
impacts.negativeImplications: Universities and other patent holders asserting software-related patents face heightened § 101 eligibility risk even after surviving pre-trial challenges, and cannot rely on worldwide sales to inflate domestic damages awards.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming server-side methods of tailoring content presentation specifications to wireless device capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming systems and methods for a server to tailor content specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities were ineligible under § 101, reversing the district court's denial of Netflix's eligibility challenge. The court reversed the infringement judgment and entered judgment for Netflix, expressly vacating rulings on inducement and certain damages evidence.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming services and technology companies gain stronger defenses against patent claims directed to adaptive content delivery, reinforcing that conventional server-side tailoring of content to device capabilities is abstract.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with portfolios covering content adaptation and streaming technology face increased invalidation risk under § 101.

impacts.caseName: VLSI Technology LLC v. Intel Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in granting summary judgment of noninfringement of a multicore processor patent on extraterritoriality grounds and in striking plaintiff's damages expert theories.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed-in-part the district court's grant of summary judgment of noninfringement to Intel on extraterritoriality grounds for VLSI's '836 patent relating to multicore processor task allocation, while affirming on other grounds. The court also addressed the propriety of striking damages expert theories for inadequate disclosure in damages contentions. The case was remanded for further proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders asserting semiconductor and processor patents benefit from a narrowing of the extraterritoriality defense, preserving domestic infringement claims even where some accused activities occur abroad.
impacts.negativeImplications: Chip manufacturers and technology companies may face broader domestic patent exposure for globally distributed product design and manufacturing processes.

impacts.caseName: International Medical Devices, Inc. v. Cornell
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether there was legally sufficient evidence to support a jury finding that asserted trade secrets related to a cosmetic penile implant were protectable trade secrets under California law, and whether inventorship claims based on those same ideas could establish patent invalidity.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the jury's trade secret misappropriation verdict, holding that the plaintiffs failed to show that each of the four asserted trade secrets was protectable because the underlying ideas were generally known. The court also reversed the patent invalidity finding premised on the same ideas constituting inventive contributions, while affirming the counterfeiting verdict related to unauthorized trademark use.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants in trade secret cases benefit from rigorous scrutiny of whether asserted secrets are truly protectable, particularly where the underlying concepts are generally known in the industry.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies relying on trade secret protection for product designs and methods must ensure robust evidence that the specific information claimed as secret is not generally known, or risk losing both trade secret and related patent claims.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims related to patents on coiled tubing products for the oil and gas industry.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgments on both inequitable conduct (in Global Tubing's favor) and Walker Process fraud (in Tenaris's favor), finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The case involved Tenaris's alleged failure to disclose prior CYMAX documents during patent prosecution and a corresponding claim of attempted monopolization. The court remanded for trial on both issues.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers benefit from the court's recognition that Walker Process fraud claims—antitrust claims based on fraud before the PTO—should not be resolved on summary judgment where material facts about intent and knowledge are disputed.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face increased litigation risk as both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims will more readily proceed to trial when underlying factual disputes exist about knowledge and intent during prosecution.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a Rule 30(b)(6) corporate representative may testify beyond personal knowledge for summary judgment purposes, and whether the on-sale bar under pre-AIA 35 U.S.C. § 102(b) requires express disclosure of a prior art device's functionality.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment of invalidity under the on-sale bar, holding that Rule 30(b)(6) testimony is admissible at summary judgment even when the corporate representative lacks personal knowledge, and rejecting the argument that on-sale bar prior art must expressly disclose claimed functionality. The court also held that computer source code constituting machine commands is not hearsay.
impacts.positiveImplications: Accused infringers benefit from an expanded evidentiary toolkit for invalidity defenses, including broader use of 30(b)(6) testimony and source code evidence, and from rejection of a heightened disclosure requirement for the on-sale bar.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face greater vulnerability to on-sale bar challenges where third-party products were sold even if the specific functionality was not publicly disclosed, and may face 30(b)(6) testimony from witnesses without direct knowledge.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a respondent in an ITC proceeding may obtain a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) of a refiled declaratory judgment action after missing the deadline for a stay in a previously filed action involving the same parties and issues.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that an ITC respondent (BioMarin) cannot circumvent the 30-day deadline for seeking a mandatory stay under § 1659(a) by voluntarily dismissing its first declaratory judgment action and refiling a new one to reset the clock. This prevents gamesmanship in coordinating parallel ITC and district court patent proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies seeking declaratory judgments of non-infringement or asserting safe harbor defenses benefit from certainty that opponents cannot manipulate procedural deadlines to delay district court resolution.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents who miss the § 1659(a) stay deadline lose the ability to obtain a mandatory stay through refiling, requiring more careful procedural planning in parallel ITC/district court proceedings.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denial of inter partes review institution constitute a 'general statement of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing discretionary IPR non-institution decisions (including the Fintiv framework) are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking. The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution and the Director retains ultimate authority over institution decisions, making these internal guidance documents rather than binding legislative rules.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's continued ability to issue and update discretionary denial policies without the delay and constraints of formal rulemaking, preserving existing Fintiv-type frameworks that may result in fewer IPR institutions.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies relying on IPR as a cost-effective mechanism to challenge patents face continued uncertainty from discretionary denial policies that can be changed without public input, potentially limiting access to IPR proceedings.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations is consistent with the governing statute, and whether 19 U.S.C. § 1516a(2)(b) abrogates the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's holding that the ITC's blanket practice of designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the statute, and that § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial records. The decision requires the ITC to assess confidentiality on a case-by-case basis consistent with statutory standards.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and domestic industries gain greater transparency in trade remedy proceedings, with improved access to information underlying ITC determinations that may affect antidumping and countervailing duties.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting questionnaire responses to the ITC face increased risk that competitively sensitive business information may become publicly accessible, potentially requiring more careful strategic management of trade remedy filings.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a damages remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated the grant of summary judgment to Microsoft based on absence of a remedy. The court held that the district court erred in its Daubert analysis of the damages expert's methodology for quantifying benefits of Microsoft's alleged infringement of network optimization patents, and should have allowed discovery reopening.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from a more permissive standard for damages expert admissibility, reducing the risk that technical or methodological critiques lead to complete exclusion and summary judgment on remedy grounds.
impacts.negativeImplications: Large technology companies face increased difficulty in using Daubert motions as a dispositive tool to eliminate patent damages claims at the summary judgment stage.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTAB erred in its construction of 'encryption information' in claims directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether that construction affected the obviousness analysis.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the PTAB's claim construction of 'encryption information' in DivX's patent on streaming encrypted media, vacated the Board's decision rejecting Netflix's obviousness challenge, and remanded for reconsideration. The decision affects streaming technology patents and the scope of IPR challenges in the adaptive bitrate streaming space.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming services and technology companies benefit from a corrected claim construction that may broaden the prior art available for obviousness challenges to DRM-related streaming patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with DRM and streaming encryption portfolios face renewed validity challenges based on broader claim constructions that may capture more prior art.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC properly found that Apple Watches with blood oxygen measurement functionality infringed Masimo's wearable pulse oximetry patents and correctly determined Masimo satisfied the domestic industry requirement.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated § 337 of the Tariff Act by importing and selling Apple Watch models that infringed Masimo's patents covering wearable blood oxygen measurement technology, upholding the limited exclusion order barring importation. The court found no error in the domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with domestic manufacturing and R&D operations benefit from the affirmed domestic industry standard and strong ITC enforcement against infringing imports of consumer electronics.
impacts.negativeImplications: Major consumer electronics companies face significant product import bans when ITC exclusion orders are upheld, potentially disrupting product lines and requiring costly design-arounds for patented features.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER operations.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's approval of a $125 million nationwide class action settlement resolving claims that PACER fees were illegally used to fund non-PACER expenses in violation of the E-Government Act. The court upheld the settlement terms, attorneys' fee awards, and incentive awards to class representatives, noting hundreds of thousands of PACER users will be fully or substantially reimbursed.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses and law firms that are frequent PACER users benefit from reimbursement of excessive fees, and the decision validates the use of class action settlements to resolve claims against the federal government for systemic overcharging.
